title: PPT Agent Quality Loop: From 67% to 72%, Then Hitting the Wall
summary: Ran 12 ticks of OPC loop to push PPT agent scores from 67% to 72%. Then discovered prompt optimization had reached 94% of theoretical maximum—the remaining 6% requires fixing rubric, template, and infra. A real accounting of agent quality engineering.
body:
## Starting with a Stuck Process

2 AM. Evaluator process stuck at 0% CPU. Logs frozen at "Executing tasks against Mitsein API..." No timeout, no errors, just hanging.

This was tick 6 of the PPT agent quality loop, running baseline eval. The first 5 ticks went suspiciously smooth—fix regression, design 10 test cases, build CLI harness, code review—all green lights. Then when it came time to actually measure quality, infra failed first.

## The httpx Ghost

Spent 30 minutes chasing root cause. Stack trace pointed to `httpcore._async/http_proxy.py`, httpx routing through HTTP proxy. But I hadn't set `HTTP_PROXY` environment variable.

Key discovery: `httpx._utils.get_environment_proxies()` doesn't just read environment variables—it reads **macOS system proxy settings**. My Clash proxy running on `127.0.0.1:7890`, configured in macOS System Preferences Web Proxy. httpx faithfully routed `localhost:8000` requests to Clash—which obviously didn't know how to handle requests to localhost.

Fix was simple: `NO_PROXY="localhost,127.0.0.1"`. But the bug's lesson wasn't simple: **macOS system proxy is invisible**. Your `env | grep -i proxy` comes up clean, httpx still uses proxy.

## Designing 10 Test Cases

PPT agent workflow: user describes needs → agent understands → generates HTML slides. 8 evaluation dimensions:

| Dimension | Weight | What it tests |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| tool_usage | 5% | Tool call correctness |
| grounding | 10% | Content has sources |
| correctness | 20% | Task completion |
| completeness | 20% | Content completeness |
| relevance | 15% | Relevance |
| format | 5% | Format compliance |
| document_quality | 15% | HTML structure and usability |
| design_quality | 10% | Visual aesthetics |

10 cases covering: basic PPT (3), data-driven (1), design-heavy (1), Chinese content (1), vague input (2), companion delegate (1), clarify flow (1).

## Baseline: 67%

P2.1 numbers:

- tool_usage: ~100% (fixed, can't go wrong)
- grounding: 0% (always 0%, explained later)
- relevance: 93% (solid)
- completeness: 86% (decent)
- correctness: 78% (needs improvement)
- design_quality: 56% (poor)
- document_quality: 50% (very poor)

Weighted average 67%. Three dimensions dragging down: correctness (task understanding not precise enough), design_quality (HTML layout not attractive), document_quality (content not deep enough).

## Prompt Surgery

5 files, pure prompt changes:

**SYSTEM.md** — Added three rules:
1. Task adherence: every user requirement must map to specific slide
2. Content depth: each slide needs at least 3 substantive points
3. Design quality: whitespace rules, visual hierarchy requirements

**SKILL.md** — Added delivery sanity check:
- Slide count must match requirements
- Every requirement must have corresponding slide
- Content can't be placeholders

**generate_prompt.j2 / system_context.j2 / think_prompt.j2** — Three Jinja2 templates synced, strengthening content depth and visual approach instructions.

No code logic changes, no tool definition changes, no HTML template changes. Pure prompt engineering.

## First Validation: The 3-Task Trap

Eager to see results, ran 3 tasks first (ppt_001, ppt_004, ppt_006). Results were confusing:

- ppt_001: document_quality jumped from 40% to 100% (too good, suspicious)
- ppt_004: design_quality dropped from 60% to 40% (regression?)
- ppt_006: correctness dropped from 85% to 75% (regression?)

3 samples + high variance of LLM scoring = noise drowning signal. One outlier can flip conclusions. This became a memex lesson: **LLM-as-judge needs at least 7 comparable tasks to detect 5-point signals**.

## Full Validation: +4.9 Points

10 tasks completed, ~55 minutes. Data from 7 comparable tasks was clear:

| Dimension | Before | After | Delta |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|
| correctness | 77.9% | **84.1%** | **+6.3%** |
| design_quality | 55.7% | **61.4%** | **+5.7%** |
| document_quality | 50.0% | **55.4%** | **+5.4%** |
| completeness | 86.3% | 88.9% | +2.6% |
| format | 81.9% | 84.3% | +2.4% |

All three target dimensions improved, consistent direction. Overall +4.9 points to 71.9%.

Biggest single-task gain: ppt_009 from 61.1% to 72.3% (+11.2%)—a "vague input" case where prompt changes helped agent better understand ambiguous requirements.

## Hitting the Wall: Why 95% is Impossible

Goal was 95%. Actually hit 72%. Missing 23 points? No, missing structure, not prompt.

**Grounding Always 0% (10% weight)**

Grounding checker logic: compare conversation text with search tool outputs, see if agent's answers are grounded. Problem is PPT agent's content lives in **HTML tool outputs**, not conversation text. Checker can't see slide content, so always gives 0.

This isn't an agent problem, it's a rubric bug.

**Document Quality Ceiling ~60%**

`document_content` checker uses Claude to evaluate HTML slide structure and usability. When HTML template itself only has basic layout (no multi-column, no mixed text/images, no card layout), no amount of prompting agent for good content will score high.

**Design Quality Ceiling ~65%**

`document_taste` checker evaluates visual aesthetics. CSS is fixed (default fonts, default spacing, default colors), prompt only controls whitespace and hierarchy—underlying design capability is template-locked.

**Two Infra Disasters (ppt_008: 13.5%, ppt_010: 22.5%)**

These tasks use companion → delegate flow. Evaluator's async client has bug handling delegation chains—`_follow_delegations()` loses thread context in certain cases.

## Ceiling Calculation

```
Non-grounding dimensions theoretical max: ~85%
With grounding (10% weight always 0):
  85% × 0.9 + 0% × 0.1 = 76.5%
Current: 71.9%
71.9 / 76.5 = 94% of theoretical max
```

Prompt optimization has reached 94%. Remaining 6% is noise and marginal effects. Continuing prompt tuning has ROI approaching zero.

## Four Real Levers

1. **Fix grounding rubric** (+7-10 points): Make checker extract slide content from HTML tool outputs, not just conversation text
2. **Fix evaluator infra** (unlock ppt_008/010): Fix `_follow_delegations()` companion-delegate handling
3. **Upgrade HTML templates** (+5-10 points document/design): Multi-column layout, card components, typography scale, color system
4. **Reduce eval variance**: Multiple runs averaged, or replace LLM judge with rules for deterministic dimensions

These four combined could theoretically push scores from 72% to 85-90%. But each requires structural change, not something one prompt can solve.

## OPC Loop Execution Log

12 ticks, P0.1 to P3.3:

| Tick | Unit | What happened |
|------|------|---------------|
| 1 | P0.1 | Fix `MAX_QUESTIONS_TASK` regression |
| 2 | P0.2 | Fix evaluator pipeline (phantom tools, cache_control) |
| 3 | P1.1 | Design 10 test cases |
| 4 | P1.2 | Build CLI test harness |
| 5 | P1.3 | Independent review |
| 6 | P2.1 | Baseline eval (67%) |
| 7 | P2.2 | Prompt improvements (5 files) |
| 8 | P2.3 | Full 10-task eval (71.9%) |
| 9 | P2.4 | Decision gate: PROCEED |
| 10 | P3.1 | SKIP (entry point testing) |
| 11 | P3.2 | SKIP (UI screenshots) |
| 12 | P3.3 | Final report |

Two skips were reasonable—eval suite already covered API paths, no UI code changes meant no screenshots needed.

One harness lesson: context compaction causes tick cursor drift. Manually changing `loop-state.json` status field then calling `complete-tick` gets rejected—harness owns state transitions, don't bypass it.

## The Real Takeaway

This isn't a success story—target 95%, actual 72%. But this is **correct measurement**.

Before running this loop, PPT agent quality was a black box. "Feels okay" "seems not great"—no numbers. Now there are:

- 8 dimensions of quantified scoring
- 10 cases of coverage
- Before/after control groups
- Clear ceiling analysis
- Four improvement levers ranked by ROI

Difference between knowing 72% and not knowing 72%: **you can make rational priority decisions**. Next step isn't more prompt tuning (because 94%), it's fixing grounding rubric (+10 points, highest ROI).

Core of quality engineering isn't making numbers bigger. It's making numbers **measurable, attributable, actionable**.
